title: A Snapshot of Influence: A Local Data Attribution Framework for Online Reinforcement Learning

reviews.summary: This paper introduces a local data attribution framework for online reinforcement learning, instantiated for PPO. It defines per-record influence scores via gradient similarity between a target function (either action log-probability or a return-surrogate based on advantages under a round-specific reference policy) and the PPO per-sample loss, computed over a single training round. The authors leverage this framework to analyze learning dynamics, perform targeted single-round interventions, and develop Iterative Influence-based Filtering (IIF), which removes negatively influential records each round and yields improved learning speed and final performance across classic control, navigation, driving, locomotion tasks, and RLHF detoxification of a 2.7B-parameter LLM.
strengths: Introduces a principled “local” data attribution formulation tailored to the circular data–model dependency in online RL, by restricting analysis to within-round effects where the data distribution is fixed.

Proposes a tractable and meaningful return-target f_return that aligns with policy gradient but stabilizes evaluation via a round-specific reference policy and advantage estimates; this target is a reusable contribution independent of the specific attribution method.
Adapts TracIn to the online RL setting, with computationally efficient design choices (single-snapshot gradients and ghost dot products), and extends the idea to sequence-level RLHF.

Applies the framework to varied environments (discrete/continuous, navigation/control/driving/locomotion) with multiple seeds, and reports interpretable measurements (sample-efficiency, run-time, returns).

Demonstrates three concrete applications: diagnostic insights (bottom records linked to inaccurate advantages), temporal behavior formation (phase transitions quantified via graph roughness), and single-round interventions that consistently uplift returns.
Presents IIF with systematic comparisons against PPO and random filtering; includes ablations (p-selection) and heuristic baselines in appendices.
Extends and validates the approach in RLHF, using an external toxicity detector and a separate test set to avoid reward hacking.
The problem setting, challenges of online RL attribution, and the rationale for local attribution are clearly articulated.
Target functions and influence computation are precisely defined, and figures effectively illustrate both qualitative insights and quantitative gains.
Limitations are candidly discussed (SGD proxy for Adam, generalization to other RL algorithms, lack of counterfactual semantics).
Opens a credible new direction for interpretability and data-centric training in online RL, a historically challenging regime for attribution.
Provides actionable tools (IIF) that improve real training pipelines, with strong empirical gains including a notable speedup in RLHF.
weaknesses: The approximation to TracIn using only the initial round checkpoint could bias influence scores; a quantitative validation (e.g., correlation with multi-checkpoint TracIn, or sensitivity across a few intermediate steps) is missing from the main text.
The PPO clipped objective is piecewise and epoch-based; using per-sample gradients at the very start of a round may not reflect the dynamics of multi-epoch optimization with clipping engaged later.

Using on-policy advantages for both training and validation blurs the boundary between “attribution” and “gradient-alignment-based sample selection”; a clearer theoretical link or guarantees would strengthen claims.

More comprehensive comparisons to principled heuristics (e.g., TD-error or advantage-based filtering) are relegated to the appendix; presenting at least one strong heuristic in the main text would calibrate IIF’s added value.
For RLHF, only toxicity is reported; helpfulness/fluency/faithfulness trade-offs and reward over-optimization checks (e.g., held-out RM variants, agreement with human evals) are not provided.
The choice of p (drop percentage) appears task-dependent; clearer guidance or adaptive schemes would help practitioners.
Exploration impacts are not deeply analyzed; aggressively filtering negative-influence samples might suppress useful exploration in sparse or deceptive-reward tasks.
“Ghost dot product” is referenced but not explained in the main text; a brief description would improve reproducibility without requiring appendix diving.

Some derivations stop short of offering intuition (e.g., how f_return at the round start essentially reduces to gradient alignment with the aggregate ascent direction when ratios ≈ 1); adding such insight would aid readers.

Limited discussion of gradient-alignment or influence-guided data selection in supervised learning and their analogs in RL beyond PER; situating IIF relative to these could clarify novelty in selection criteria versus classic prioritization.
Connections to on-policy sample reweighting or variance-reduction techniques in policy gradient methods are not explicitly discussed.
reviews.questions:
1. How well do influence scores computed from a single checkpoint correlate with those computed across multiple within-round checkpoints (e.g., Spearman rank of bottom/top sets)? Can you report this for a couple of environments?
2. Does IIF retain its gains when using Adam (or commonly used RLHF optimizers) rather than SGD? If not, can you discuss practical adaptations for adaptive optimizers?
3. How sensitive are the gains to the quality of advantage estimates (e.g., varying GAE λ, bootstrap value accuracy) and to reward sparsity? Any results on very sparse-reward tasks?
4. Can you include in the main text at least one strong heuristic baseline (advantage- or TD-error-based filtering) and summarize how IIF compares? Are there cases where heuristics perform similarly or better?
5. In RLHF, besides toxicity, what happens to helpfulness/utility metrics (e.g., win-rate vs. a helpfulness RM, or human preference) to rule out regressions? Any evidence of reward over-optimization or mode collapse?
6. Could you briefly explain the “ghost dot product” in the main text and clarify the memory/runtime trade-offs relative to storing per-sample gradients?
7. Have you explored adaptive p (e.g., based on influence distribution statistics or a validation return threshold) to reduce hyperparameter tuning and prevent over-pruning in exploration-heavy regimes?
reviews.assessment: This paper makes a timely and substantial contribution by introducing a local data attribution perspective for online RL and demonstrating its utility for analysis and training. The framework is conceptually clean, the target functions—especially f_return—are thoughtfully designed, and the empirical results are strong across diverse tasks, including RLHF. The IIF algorithm is simple, practical, and effective. The primary weaknesses are the reliance on a single-checkpoint approximation without a main-text validation, limited discussion of exploration impacts, and relatively narrow RLHF evaluation metrics. Addressing these points—particularly a correlation study for influence approximations, a stronger in-main-text heuristic baseline, and broader RLHF metrics—would further solidify the case. Overall, I recommend acceptance; the ideas are original, well-motivated, and valuable to the NeurIPS community.
